questions.question: Explain the difference between shell variables and environment variables in Linux.
keyPoints: Shell variables are local to the current shell only
Environment variables are exported and inherited by child processes
Use 'export' command to convert shell variable to environment variable
Use 'set' to view shell variables, 'env' for environment variables

questions.question: List and explain four commonly used environment variables in Linux with their purposes.
keyPoints: PATH - directories searched for executables
HOME - user's home directory
USER - current username
SHELL - default shell interpreter
PWD - current working directory
PS1 - command prompt format

questions.question: Explain the use of the 'export' command in Linux with an example.
keyPoints: export makes shell variables available to child processes
Syntax: export VAR_NAME or VAR_NAME=value; export VAR_NAME
Variables must be exported to be available in subshells and scripts
Without export, variables remain local to current shell

questions.question: Describe the difference between .bashrc and .bash_profile files.
keyPoints: .bashrc is executed for interactive non-login shells
.bash_profile is executed for login shells
New terminal windows typically source .bashrc
Settings in .bash_profile run once at login
Both are used for environment customization

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the PATH environment variable and how it is used by the shell.
keyPoints: PATH contains colon-separated list of directories
Shell searches directories in PATH order when executing commands
If command not found in PATH, 'command not found' error occurs
PATH can be modified to add custom program locations
Use echo $PATH to view current PATH value

questions.question: What is the purpose of the $? variable? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: $? stores exit status of last executed command
Value 0 indicates successful execution
Non-zero values indicate failure
Useful for error checking in shell scripts
Example: mkdir test; echo $? (returns 0 if successful)

questions.question: How do you set a permanent environment variable in Linux? Explain the procedure.
keyPoints: Add export statements to ~/.bashrc for non-login shells
Add to ~/.bash_profile for login shells
Syntax: export VARIABLE_NAME=value
Source the file after editing: source ~/.bashrc
Use ~/.profile for broader compatibility

questions.question: Explain the concept of environment variables in Linux/Unix. Discuss their types, purposes, and how they are managed. Include commands to display, set, and unset environment variables.
keyPoints: Environment variables are dynamic values affecting process behavior
Types: System-defined, User-defined, Shell variables
Common commands: env, printenv, export, unset, set
System-wide variables in /etc/environment or /etc/profile
User-specific in ~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_profile
Process of inheritance from parent to child processes
Practical examples of setting and using variables

questions.question: Write a shell script that demonstrates the use of environment variables. The script should show how to access, set, and export variables, and explain the difference between local and environment variables.
keyPoints: Script demonstrating variable access ($VAR syntax)
Setting variables and exporting them
Demonstrating child process visibility
Using env command to verify
Comparison between exported and non-exported variables
Practical examples with HOME, PATH, USER
Exit status handling with $?

questions.question: Explain the Linux shell initialization files. Discuss the sequence in which .bash_profile, .bashrc, .profile, and /etc/profile are executed, and how environment variables are configured permanently.
keyPoints: Login shell vs non-login shell initialization
Sequence: /etc/profile → ~/.bash_profile → ~/.bash_login → ~/.profile
Interactive non-login sources .bashrc
/etc/bash.bashrc for system-wide bashrc
Setting PATH, aliases, and custom variables
Environment variable persistence across sessions
Best practices for configuration

questions.question: Discuss the significance of PATH, HOME, and SHELL environment variables. Also explain how you would modify PATH to include a custom directory for user-installed programs.
keyPoints: PATH: Command search path, colon-separated directories
HOME: User's home directory, default for cd command
SHELL: Default shell interpreter
PATH modification using export PATH=$PATH:/custom/directory
Making PATH change permanent in .bashrc
Security considerations with PATH
Demonstration of order of directory search in PATH
